17.7 Alert Monitor
The alert monitor in PP/DS is a tool that a planner can use during planning to manage
and evaluate exception messages centrally. Messages about problems are transferred
automaticallly from the application to the alert monitor, and each exception message
relates to a specific problem situation. The primary task of the alert monitor is to list
exception messages in a structured manner, and the messages correspond to the
defined selection criteria. The alert monitor provides information on situations that
require adjustments in planning. Note the distinction between the reason for the
exception and its properties. Using exception messages, the alert monitor can provide
general information on the problem situation itself but not necessarily on why the
exception occurred.
Let’s first discuss how to set up the alert monitor before showing how it works in plan-
ning and scheduling.
To create a new alert monitor profile, you use Transaction /SAPAPO/AMON1 and
choose Alert Profile on the initial screen. Figure 17.51 shows alert profile ZPPDSALTMON,
in which checking the relevant boxes in the Selection column will trigger relevant
alerts. You can also define ranges or specific values so that the system brings up infor-
mation, warnings, or error alerts upon meeting the specified values. In this example,
the Product Too Early (Fixed Pegging) alert type shows alert thresholds for information,
warnings, and error alerts.
Note
After you create an alert profile, you assign it to an overall profile so that the system
can display alerts within an application (refer to Figure 17.27 to see how defined alert

17.8 Plan Monitor
The plan monitor evaluates the key figures for a plan in relation to specific objects, ver-
sions, and periods, and it then assigns scores to the results. A planner can define their
own calculation rules according to which the scores are to be calculated, or they can
simply use standard aggregations (e.g., sum, mean value).
The plan monitor can be used to do the following:
 Determine the quality of the current planning situation.
 Compare various planning and simulation versions with each other.
 Compare planning over different periods.
The plan monitor considers the key figures for which a planner has created key figure
variants in the key figure schema. These key figures can be displayed in a tabular or
graphical version or both.
For example, say a production planner plans over a three- to four-week horizon but is
faced with new expectations every day that impact many areas of the plan over the
upcoming weeks. Here, the plan monitor can help.
Key figures in the following areas of the plan monitor are available:
 Key figures for order quantities
 Key figures for order times
 Key figures for pegging relationships
 Key figures for stocks
 Key figures for resources

17.9 Summary
17.9 Summary
In this chapter, we covered important configurations, master data, business processes,
and reporting of PP/DS in SAP S/4HANA. The PP/DS optimizer attempts to maintain a
balance between costs and benefits for producing or not producing products and to
determine where trade-offs are necessary. The user-defined alerts in the alert monitor
and monitoring key figures in the plan monitor keep various checks and balances in
place for smoother production and procurement planning. SAP Fiori apps for PP/DS
not only report via dashboards but also enable intuitive planning.

Chapter 18
Production Engineering and
Operations
SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for production engineering and
operations is one of the latest additions to the manufacturing space
in SAP S/4HANA as an embedded solution. It caters to large, complex,
turnkey projects, such as those in the aerospace, aircraft, shipbuilding,
defense, and space industries, and it’s an extension of discrete manufac-
turing production types.
SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for production engineering and operations is gaining
significant traction in complex, low-volume, and extended manufacturing processes,
and it’s an extension of discrete manufacturing processes. Due to the extensive design
and development stages involved in a complex, large-scale, turnkey manufacturing
process, production engineering and operations attempts to bring a greater balance
between design changes (engineering bills of materials [EBOMs]) and their impact on
operations/manufacturing (manufacturing bills of materials [MBOMs]).
Here are some of the traits to consider when deciding whether to implement produc-
tion engineering and operations:
 Highly engineered products
 Engineer-to-order (ETO)
 Make-to-order (MTO and configure-to-order
 Complex assembly production
 Low volume of orders
 High numbers of engineering change requests
 Labor-intensive processes
 Long cycle times (days, weeks, and months)
 High numbers of components and operations
Materials with some or all of the above traits are prime candidates to be considered for
production via production engineering and operations.

18.1 Key Terminology
Then, we discuss the system configuration, roles, and authorizations required for using
production engineering and operations. Then, we explain the core business processes of
production engineering and the master data setup requirements and version manage-
ment that support the production engineering and operations processes. We provide
comparisons to demonstrate key differences between the master data setup required for
production engineering and operations and the master data setup required for standard
discrete manufacturing. We then use an example to walk you through the process of
transitioning from engineering to manufacturing execution operations and using tools
to hand over processes from product lifecycle management systems to SAP S/4HANA
systems. Then, we discuss the business processes of production operations in a step-by-
step manner. Furthermore, we briefly touch upon integration of production engineering
and operations with other SAP S/4HANA areas, such as materials management, quality
management, the SAP Document Management service, and SAP Human Capital Manage-
ment for SAP S/4HANA. Lastly, as production engineering and operations processes
entail significant engineering changes to the products being manufactured, we also dis-
cuss change management and impact analysis.
Note
Because production engineering and operations processes significantly resemble dis-
crete manufacturing processes, we recommend that you read Chapter 3 and Chapter 6
before reading this chapter. We’ll only be discussing the processes briefly here so as to
stay focused on production engineering and operations.
18.1 Key Terminology
In Figure 18.2 you’ll see some of the following key terminologies used in production
engineering and operations:
 Production engineering
This bridges the gap between product engineering and production operations by
converting the product design into production process design, which is the basis for
production order management and shop floor execution.
 Manufacturing change management
This orchestrates the end-to-end production engineering process, as follows:
– The Monitor Engineering Snapshots app informs you of new sets of EBOM ver-
sions and triggers the production engineering process.
– The Manufacturing Change Record orchestrates the whole production engineer-
ing process and keeps track of all changes made to manufacturing objects when
you’re incorporating a new EBOM version.

18 Production Engineering and Operations
960
– The Change Impact Analysis app identifies all objects that are impacted by a prod-
uct engineering change.
– BOM and routing version management control the lifecycle and usage of differ-
ent revision levels.
 SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner
You use this to accomplish the handover from the EBOM to the MBOM by convert-
ing the design-driven EBOM structure into a manufacturing, built-sequence-driven
MBOM structure.
 Production process planning
This is used to create shop floor routings that include all information needed for
production planning, production costing, and shop floor execution, as follows:
– Operations are used for planning and costing purposes, as in classic SFC and dis-
crete manufacturing.
– Operation activities are used for shop floor execution, and they describe in detail
how an operation has to be performed: with work instructions, data collection
required, qualifications needed, production resources/tools (PRT), and buyoff
procedures.
Production process planning includes a set of “supporting” features:
– The BOMs and routings used for planning (MRP and PP/DS) can be distinguished
from those used for shop floor execution. The usage of BOMs and routings for dif-
ferent purposes is defined in the Manage Production Version app.
– Using templates and favorites significantly increases user efficiency when main-
taining routings.
– In the Manage Standard Texts app, a manufacturing engineer can maintain a
library of reusable text elements for efficient work instruction authoring.
– Buyoff templates can be defined and used during routing maintenance as an
approval process.
– Qualification requirements can be predefined for work centers and materials and
are inherited by related operations and activities.

form so you can use production engineering and operations.

18.2.10 Planning Scenarios
The next configuration step lets you activate or deactivate the planning scenarios for
creating a planning scope alternative. A planning scenario defines the parameters and
the associated scope based on which the system undertakes planning in production
engineering and operations. Here are some of the important planning scenarios that
you can create, activate or deactivate:
 Document-based nonconfigurable
This scenario allows a planner to create a planning scope alternative in which the
EBOM is derived from the document linked to an RH file and the target is a noncon-
figurable structure. We’ll use this planning scenario as an example to demonstrate
the end-to-end business process in this chapter.
 Document-based purchased part
This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative for a purchased part.
The planning scope alternative of a purchased part is not available for planning.
 Product structure manager-based configurable
This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is
derived from the product structure manager and the target is a configurable struc-
ture.

to the network activity. The reference points are copied from network activity to the

18.5 Production Operations
Production operations are about initiating, preparing, executing, and documenting the
actual end-to-end production process. A shop floor production order is an extended

18 Production Engineering and Operations
990
version of a PP production order in discrete manufacturing. We’ll cover many additional
details of shop floor production orders in production engineering and operations here.
Figure 18.43 shows the end-to-end process flow that we’ll discuss in these sections,
along with the following additional details:
 Operations are used for planning and costing, as in classic SFC.
 Activities are used for shop floor execution, and they describe in detail how an oper-
ation has to be performed—that is, work instructions, data collection required, qual-
ifications needed, PRTs, buyoff procedures, and so on.
 Production monitoring provides a comprehensive overview of the expected and
actual status and progress of production orders and operations by automatically
detecting exceptions, providing decision support, and allowing for immediate
actions.
 Engineering change incorporates and updates production orders with the latest
engineering changes.
 Complex assembly execution is primarily designed for complex, low-volume pro-
duction processes with high change frequency and a focus on manual work—which
is typical for complex assembly and engineer-to-order businesses.
 Work assignment and work queues orchestrate the actual execution of production
order operations and activities. Operators can access their tasks in a work center’s
work queues, or supervisors can explicitly distribute work to their team members by
moving tasks to personal work queues.
 The Perform Work app supports production operators in executing their tasks by
guiding them through the production process with the help of interactive work
instructions, 3D visual support, data collection with real-time valuations, and so on.

18.5.2 Assign Work App
The Assign Work app lists all operations to be performed at a given work center. It sup-
ports the operator in picking the next operation to be worked on based on order and
operation status, priority, scheduled start/end, start feasibility, and issues such as
holds, missing components, and delays (see Figure 18.45). Production supervisors can
see all their operators with their currently assigned workloads and labor statuses.
Supervisors can assign additional operation activities to operators while considering
their qualifications and work center assignments. Supervisors can also remove opera-
tion activities from the operator’s worklist and maintain target times for each opera-
tion activity assigned to an operator.
Some additional available functions are as follows:
 Select all unassigned operation activities or check existing assignments.
 Assign one or more production operators to individual operation activities to dis-
tribute the workload.
 Filter operators’ names by matching work centers and qualifications during an
assignment.
 View a number of current operators’ assignments.
 Add and edit the target duration for an operation activity during an assignment.
 Unassign operators from operation activities.
 Assign (and unassign) not only operators but also teams to specific operations.

18.5.5 Change Impact Analysis App
Before we delve into discussing the Change Impact Analysis app, let’s briefly discuss
another SAP Fiori app: Manage Change Records. You can create a change record at any
stage of the production engineering and operations business process by using the Man-
age Change Record app. However, we highly recommend that you to create a change
record as early as possible so that all objects affected by the changes are part of the
change record. The primary purpose of a change record is to contain references to all the
objects that are relevant to the change cycle. The following objects are supported:
 Master data: EBOM, MBOM, shop floor routing, and planning scope
 Transactional data: production order and purchase order
Change items can be configured depending on the change record type. You indicate the
context of the object in the change record by using the change relevance setting—for
example, Create, Change, New Version, Reference, and so on. The status network of the
change record is also configurable.
A manufacturing engineer can use the change record as a single point of entry to access
all the relevant objects for the change cycle. The change record enables an engineer to
navigate to the corresponding apps to view or manipulate the objects.
You access the screen shown in Figure 18.56 by opening the Manage Engineering
Records app or the Manage Change Records app, followed by selecting change record
200000020. This is the same change record that you created while creating the shop
floor routing. More objects, such as planning scopes, manufacturing material BOMs,
and even production order 80000043 were added to this change record later on to ana-
lyze the impact of changes made to any or all of these objects. Then, choose Analyze
Impact on the screen shown in Figure 18.56.
The change impact analysis can be triggered from different master data objects: EBOMs,
MBOMs, and shop floor routings. It determines the net change of the trigger objects
and identifies all manufacturing objects that are impacted by those changes: MBOMs,

19.1 The Basics of Reporting
To fully take advantage of SAP S/4HANA, it’s essential to become familiar with the
icons and other features frequently available for use in reports. Because these icons
and functions are the same in all the information systems and analyses, a user can nav-
igate faster, get to desired information more quickly, and even find tips and tricks to
manage the display and availability of data as needed.
Note
It’s important to note that you may find similar icons in master data and transactions
such as the REM planning table (in Transaction MF50) or the stock/requirements list (in
Transaction MD04), but these icons may have different meanings and functions. For
example, in a standard business process, clicking the Details icon brings up the Item
details screen for the object. In the reporting function, however, clicking this icon
brings up a comprehensive list titled All Key Figures.
Information systems present a large amount of data and information in logically struc-
tured lists and sections. We provide you with the following menu paths and transac-
tion codes for both discrete manufacturing and process manufacturing to make the
information systems easier to use:
 Discrete manufacturing
Choose Logistics  Production  Shop Floor Control  Information System  Order Infor-

19.2 Report Parameters
When we talk about the order information system, it can be either the production order
information system or the process order information system. The former is specific to
discrete manufacturing reporting, while the latter is for process manufacturing report-
ing.
An information system consists of the most up-to-date information about a specific
object, and it’s based on the transactions that the user has performed as part of the
business process. For example, when a planner executes the operations list in a produc-
tion order information system, the system displays the current status of various oper-
ations, including those that are completely or partially confirmed. If the same report is
run or executed again after six hours—for example, when the user has recorded more
confirmations for production orders—at that time, the system shows up-to-date (and
different) information. Another example is that when the user first uses the sales infor-
mation system, it shows the creation of 10 sales order for a specific material. After six
hours, when the user again runs the report (in the sales information system), the num-
ber of sales orders has increased to 18. This information helps to reflect the accurate
and up-to-date status of various activities and business processes.

standing and comprehension.

19.2.6 Maintain Variant
Maintaining a variant in SAP S/4HANA is similar to saving a layout that is then avail-
able for repeated use when needed. A user can also set a variant as a default by manag-
ing the layout options. For example, say that when using the order information sys-
tem, a user always uses plant 1710, order type PI01, and the date range of 30 days from
today. Instead of entering all of those parameters every time to run the order informa-
tion system, a user can simply save these details as a variant and subsequently select it.
There are two areas in which you can maintain variants. The first one is on the Initial
Parameters Selection screen of any report, information system, or analysis that you
use when defining the selection parameters. You can simply enter the information
once in the parameter selection screen and then save the variant by choosing Goto 
Variants  Save as Variant. On the Variant Attributes screen that appears, you can give
the variant a name and a short description and then choose whether to make each field
required, hidden, or protected from data entry. Then, you can save the variant, and
next time, you can simply choose Goto  Variants  Get to get the variant. You can also
delete incorrect or unwanted variants.
You can use the second option for saving a variant when an analysis report is already
executed in the order information system. The system displays all list fields, and a user
has made the required changes—for example, setting any filters, sorting any columns
in ascending or descending order, or performing aggregation. In this case, you choose
Select Layout and select the Save Layout option from the dropdown. In the ensuing
popup, you enter the name of the layout and a short description. Here again, a user can
change or delete the saved layouts, if needed.

Chapter 20
Standard Planning Analysis
Standard analyses in SAP S/4HANA provide great insights into many
important logistics and supply chain management key performance
indicators, which can help companies make informed decisions. Stan-
dard analyses aren’t just available in production planning but also in all
logistics and supply chain management areas of SAP S/4HANA.
Standard analyses enable users to draw information from the available standard info
structures in SAP S/4HANA. The difference between shop floor information system
(reports) and standard analyses is that in information systems, the information is
dynamic and changes when the user performs the relevant business functions. In stan-
dard analyses, the system instead draws from historical information to enable the user
to make better business decisions. In other words, the data and information from the
information systems flow into the analyses.
This chapter will cover some of the standard analyses for discrete manufacturing. We
also review a few features and functionalities that are available only in analyses and not
in information systems, such as time series analysis and ABC analysis. The concepts
underlying standard analyses for discrete manufacturing that we discuss in this chap-
ter will enable users to explore standard analysis of process manufacturing and REM,
for which we’ve provided the necessary menu paths as well as the associated transac-
tion codes.

tem uses for calculating the material shortages. These rules define the scope of the
shortage calculation—that is, the supply and demand elements to be considered and
the conditions that have to be met for a shortage to be relevant.

21.3 Summary
This chapter covered how to leverage some of the next-generation SAP Fiori apps for
PP. The information covered in this chapter can certainly help a planner use the same
reporting tools and features in other logistics and supply chain components of an SAP
S/4HANA system.
To conclude, PP in the SAP S/4HANA system is quite comprehensive and offers in-
depth and practical solutions for a large number of business processes and scenarios in
a diverse range of industries. It also offers options to implement and integrate PP in all
important business processes. However, your company can only ensure an actual
return on its investment in SAP S/4HANA system implementation by striving to
understand the integration of various SAP S/4HANA system areas with each other. You
can achieve flawless integration during an SAP S/4HANA system implementation by
maintaining extensive collaboration and intense coordination with the various stake-
holders of your company. In addition, those who work in supply chain management
roles have seen how the PP function is vital to many processes in the logistics and sup-
ply chain.
